ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Yong Tau Foo with laksa broth; 'stuffed tofu food product' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible anchor. Laksa broth component and mixed-piece configuration are not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — mixed stuffed tofu items in spiced coconut-based broth (laksa); FoodEx2 category A0FJP (mixed dishes, not elsewhere classified) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo laksa. A0FJP (mixed dishes NEC) is the closest defensible category. Exact code not available in published FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Yong Tau Foo or laksa-based stuffed tofu dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving of Yong Tau Foo laksa consists of 7 assorted stuffed pieces (approximately 200–220 g of solids) served in approximately 180–200 ml of laksa broth, totalling roughly 400 g per bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (Laksa, 7 pieces, mixed) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for the exact food item 'Yong Tau Foo (Laksa, 7 pieces, mixed)' (cr_id F08010067). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from HPB laksa broth composition data and coconut milk contribution (~4.2 g/100 g); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and micronutrients not individually reported in HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed tofu and fish-paste dish with coconut-milk broth
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo laksa in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) mixed tofu and fish-paste items having a low GI due to high protein and fat content moderating glucose response; (2) laksa broth contributing coconut milk fat which further blunts glycaemic response; (3) carbohydrate content of only 5.49 g/100 g (predominantly from tofu and vegetable fillings). Analogous reference: mixed tofu dishes and fish-paste products in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.96 g) / 100 ≈ 9.9, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 5.49 × 4 = 21.96 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 479 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,916 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the 2,000 mg/day recommended limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid the laksa broth, request reduced-sodium preparation, and restrict portion to 3–4 pieces to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per meal occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.49 g/100 g), low GI (~45), and moderate protein (4.53 g/100 g) collectively produce a minimal postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo laksa without added noodles or rice is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; limit broth consumption to reduce saturated fat from coconut milk, and avoid sweet chili accompaniments to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.2 g/100 g from coconut milk laksa broth, combined with high sodium (479 mg/100 g), presents a dual cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request all-boiled pieces to reduce AGE and fat exposure, limit broth consumption to reduce saturated fat and sodium load, and keep total serving to 3–4 pieces with minimal broth.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mixed preparation includes deep-fried pieces (advanced glycation end-products), sodium 479 mg/100 g exceeds 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, nightshade vegetables (eggplant, chili), and elevated saturated fat from coconut milk.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request all-boiled pieces to eliminate AGE exposure, avoid nightshade fillings (eggplant, chili), limit broth to reduce saturated fat and sodium, and treat this dish as an occasional food only.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (Laksa, 7 Pieces, Mixed) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish of Hakka Chinese origin, widely adopted across Peranakan and local Chinese food culture. The dish consists of seven assorted stuffed items — typically including tofu puffs, bitter gourd, eggplant, chili, fish balls, and fish-paste-stuffed tofu — served in a rich, spiced laksa broth made from coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, shrimp paste (belacan), dried shrimp, and candlenut. The pieces may be boiled, deep-fried, or a combination of both, depending on the hawker stall. The laksa broth is creamy, aromatic, and moderately spicy, with a characteristic coconut-curry fragrance. Nutritionally, this dish provides 103 kcal per 100 g, with moderate fat (7.1 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from coconut milk at ~4.2 g/100 g), moderate protein (4.53 g/100 g from fish paste and tofu), and low carbohydrate (5.49 g/100 g). Sodium is elevated at 479 mg/100 g due to shrimp paste and laksa seasoning, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added noodles or rice. Dietary fibre is modest at 0.75 g/100 g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to fish paste and shrimp paste content, and is not certified halal due to shrimp paste preparation practices at many hawker stalls. It is a common working lunch and hawker dinner option in Singapore, typically ordered at yong tau foo stalls where customers select their preferred pieces and broth type.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the exact food item (cr_id F08010067) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from HPB laksa broth composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to mixed tofu and fish-paste dishes with coconut-milk broth; GI confidence is moderate. Overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match for macros.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Yong Tau Foo laksa preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F08010067) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat of 4.2 g/100 g is estimated from HPB laksa broth composition data and known coconut milk contribution to laksa broth; this is an approximation and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo laksa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. The dish is classified as 'mixed' preparation as hawker stalls typically offer both boiled and fried pieces; the fried pieces contribute to AGE load relevant for rheumatoid arthritis assessment. 5. Halal status is uncertain as shrimp paste (belacan) preparation and cross-contamination practices vary by stall. 6. The input record listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; this has been corrected to false as the dish contains fish paste and shrimp paste. 7. FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible anchor for mixed dishes NEC; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Yong Tau Foo laksa.
